---
title: "Ch 5 — Linear Inequalities"
subtitle: "When the answer is not a number but a range — and how to draw it"
lesson-id: "cbse11.linear-inequalities"
tier: "cbse11"
ncert-class: 11
ncert-chapter: 5
ncert-chapter-title: "Linear Inequalities"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse10.pair-linear-equations", "t2.linear-inequalities"]
unlocks: ["cbse12.linear-programming"]
applied-domains: ["research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 40
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you solve $3x + 5 = 14$ in your head?
2. Do you remember that adding the same number to both sides of an
   equation preserves the equation?
3. Have you ever drawn the graph of $y = 2x + 1$?
4. Do the symbols $<,\ \le,\ >,\ \ge$ feel routine to you?

Three confident yeses — read on.
:::

## Touch

You have a budget of $\overline{\rule{1em}{0pt}}500$ to spend at a stationery
shop. Pens cost $\overline{\rule{1em}{0pt}}30$ each, notebooks
$\overline{\rule{1em}{0pt}}80$ each. Let $x$ be the number of pens and $y$
the number of notebooks.

How much can you spend? *At most* $500$. The constraint is not "exactly
500" — that would be an equation. It is *at most*:
$$30x + 80y \le 500.$$

The plot below shows the **feasible region** — every $(x, y)$ that
respects the budget. Drag $x$; watch the maximum $y$.

```{ojs}
viewof xPens = Inputs.range([0, 17], {value: 5, step: 1, label: "x = pens"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const x = xPens;
  const yMax = Math.floor((500 - 30 * x) / 80);
  const cost = 30 * x + 80 * yMax;
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;line-height:1.7;">
    <div>With <b>${x} pens</b> at ₹30 each (₹${30 * x} spent), the
      most notebooks you can afford at ₹80 each is <b>${yMax}</b>.</div>
    <div>Total spent: <b>₹${cost}</b> (budget ₹500).</div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.4em;color:#666;">
      The pair (${x}, ${yMax}) is on the boundary 30x + 80y ≤ 500. Every
      pair below this line is also feasible.
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

The set of all feasible $(x, y)$ is a **half-plane**, bounded by a line.
This is the chapter.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. Solving $3x > 6$ — when can you "divide both sides by 3"? Is there a
   side-effect of dividing by a *negative* number?
2. The single inequality $x \ge 0$ describes which half of the line?
3. If you have *two* inequalities, $x \ge 0$ and $y \ge 0$, what does
   their *intersection* look like in the plane?
4. Do the equality cases ($\le$ vs $<$) matter for the picture? Why
   does it become a *dashed* line for strict inequalities?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

The dangerous step is multiplying or dividing by a *negative* — the
inequality flips. Forgetting this is the single most common error in
the chapter.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — Solve a one-variable inequality

Solve $4x - 3 < 17$ for $x$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Treat it like an equation, but keep the inequality sign.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4x < 20 \Rightarrow x < 5$.

Solution set: $(-\infty, 5)$. On the number line: open circle at 5, arrow
to the left.
</details>

### Problem 2 — Negative coefficient

Solve $-2x + 5 > 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$-2x > -4$. Now divide by $-2$ — and remember to flip the inequality.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-2x > -4 \Rightarrow x < 2$ (dividing by $-2$ flips $>$ to $<$).
</details>

### Problem 3 — Combined inequality

Solve $-3 \le 2x + 1 < 7$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Subtract 1 from all three parts; then divide by 2.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-4 \le 2x < 6 \Rightarrow -2 \le x < 3$.

Solution set $[-2, 3)$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — Graph in the plane

Sketch the half-plane $y \ge 2x - 1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Draw the line $y = 2x - 1$ first. Decide which side of the line satisfies
the inequality by testing a sample point (e.g. the origin).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Line passes through $(0, -1)$ and $(1, 1)$. Test $(0, 0)$:
$0 \ge -1$? Yes. So $(0, 0)$ is in the solution. Shade the side above
the line. Boundary is *solid* because $\ge$ includes equality.
</details>

### Problem 5 — System of inequalities

Sketch the region defined by $x + y \le 4,\ x \ge 0,\ y \ge 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$x \ge 0$ and $y \ge 0$ keep you in the first quadrant. Then bound from
above by $x + y = 4$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A triangle with vertices $(0,0), (4, 0), (0, 4)$, including the
boundary.
</details>

### Problem 6 — A word problem

A factory produces two products: each unit of $A$ uses $2$ kg of raw
material; each unit of $B$ uses $3$ kg. The factory has $24$ kg of raw
material. Write the constraint.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2x + 3y \le 24$, plus $x \ge 0$, $y \ge 0$ (you can't produce negative
units).
</details>

You have just used **flipping the sign on negative multiplication**,
**combined inequalities**, **half-plane graphs**, and the **boundary
line solid/dashed convention**. Now we name them.

::: {.fun-fact}
The symbols $<$ and $>$ are surprisingly young. They were introduced by
**Thomas Harriot** in *Artis Analyticae Praxis* (printed posthumously in
1631), wedged into a manuscript of his alongside our modern equality.
For two thousand years before that, mathematicians wrote inequalities in
sentences — "minus thus is greater than" — until Harriot's two strokes
caught on. The next time you write $x \le 5$, remember it took the
Renaissance to compress that idea into three keystrokes.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A [linear inequality](/glossary.qmd#linear-inequality) is *almost* a
[linear equation](/glossary.qmd#linear-equation) — same algebra, same
graph — except the answer is a **region**, not a point. The single
algebraic difference is rule 3: multiplying or dividing by a *negative*
flips the sign. That one rule, plus "shade the half-plane that contains
a test point you trust," carries the entire chapter.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **linear inequality** in one variable has the form
$$ax + b \,\square\, 0 \quad \text{where } \square \in \{<, \le, >, \ge\}.$$

Operating rules (carry the inequality through unchanged, **except** rule 4):

1. Add or subtract the same value on both sides — sign unchanged.
2. Multiply or divide both sides by a *positive* number — sign unchanged.
3. Multiply or divide both sides by a *negative* number — **flip** the
   inequality sign.
4. The same applies in two variables: $ax + by \,\square\, c$.

**Geometric picture:**

| Inequality | Picture in $\mathbb{R}^2$ |
|:--|:--|
| $ax + by \le c$ | half-plane below/left of the line $ax+by = c$, **solid** boundary |
| $ax + by < c$ | same half-plane, **dashed** boundary |
| $ax + by \ge c$ | other half-plane, solid boundary |
| $ax + by > c$ | other half-plane, dashed boundary |

A **system of linear inequalities** has the **intersection** of the
half-planes as its solution. This region is always convex and is the
seed of *linear programming* (Class 12).

:::

A short verse:

> *Add and subtract — the sign holds true;*
> *Multiply by minus — the sign turns askew.*
> *Draw a line, pick a side, shade and seal —*
> *Strict gets a dash, weak gets the deal.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Solve and graph: $\dfrac{2x - 3}{4} + 9 \ge 3 + \dfrac{4x}{3}$.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. Multiply both sides by $12$ (LCM of denominators) to clear fractions:
   $3(2x - 3) + 108 \ge 36 + 16x$.
2. Expand: $6x - 9 + 108 \ge 36 + 16x$.
3. Simplify: $6x + 99 \ge 36 + 16x$, i.e. $63 \ge 10x$, i.e. $x \le 6.3$.

**Solution set:** $(-\infty, 6.3]$. On the number line: closed circle at
6.3, arrow to the left.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Solve $5x + 3 > 18$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5x > 15 \Rightarrow x > 3$.
</details>

**W2.** Solve $-4x + 1 \le 9$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-4x \le 8 \Rightarrow x \ge -2$ (sign flipped).
</details>

**W3.** Solve $\dfrac{x}{2} - 1 < 3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{x}{2} < 4 \Rightarrow x < 8$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Solve $\dfrac{3(x - 1)}{2} \le \dfrac{2(x + 3)}{3}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply both sides by 6 to clear fractions.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$9(x - 1) \le 4(x + 3) \Rightarrow 9x - 9 \le 4x + 12 \Rightarrow
5x \le 21 \Rightarrow x \le 21/5$.
</details>

**P2.** Solve the system: $2x + y \le 6,\ x + 2y \le 6,\ x \ge 0,\ y \ge 0$.
Sketch.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Find intersection of the two slanted lines: $2x + y = 6$ and $x + 2y = 6$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Intersection: subtract — $(2x + y) - (x + 2y) = 0$ gives $x = y$. Sub
into either: $3x = 6$, so $x = y = 2$.

Vertices of the region: $(0, 0), (3, 0), (2, 2), (0, 3)$ — a
quadrilateral.
</details>

**P3.** A man wants to cut three lengths from a $91$-cm board. The
second is to be $3$ cm longer than the first; the third is to be twice
as long as the first. What are the possible lengths of the first piece?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let first $= x$. Second $= x + 3$. Third $= 2x$. Total $\le 91$. Also
$x > 0$ and the third must be at least 5 cm longer than the second.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x + (x+3) + 2x \le 91 \Rightarrow 4x + 3 \le 91 \Rightarrow x \le 22$.

Also $2x \ge (x+3) + 5 \Rightarrow x \ge 8$.

**Answer:** $8 \le x \le 22$ (cm).
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Solve $|x - 2| < 3$ and $|2x + 5| \ge 1$ simultaneously.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$|y| < c \Leftrightarrow -c < y < c$. $|y| \ge c \Leftrightarrow y \le -c$
or $y \ge c$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
First: $-3 < x - 2 < 3 \Rightarrow -1 < x < 5$.

Second: $2x + 5 \le -1$ or $2x + 5 \ge 1$, i.e. $x \le -3$ or $x \ge -2$.

Intersection of $(-1, 5)$ with $\{x \le -3\} \cup \{x \ge -2\}$:
$(-1, 5) \cap [-2, \infty) = (-1, 5)$. (The $x \le -3$ branch
contributes nothing.)

**Answer:** $-1 < x < 5$.
</details>

**S2.** Find the region in the plane satisfying:
$x + 2y \le 8,\ 2x + y \le 8,\ x, y \ge 0$. Identify the vertices.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Intersect each pair of lines.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Intersection of slanted lines: $x + 2y = 8$, $2x + y = 8$. Subtract:
$x - y = 0$, so $x = y = 8/3$.

Vertices: $(0, 0)$, $(4, 0)$, $(8/3, 8/3)$, $(0, 4)$.
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** The solution of $-x + 2 \le 0$ is
(a) $x \le 2$ (b) $x \ge 2$ (c) $x < 2$ (d) $x > 2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-x \le -2 \Rightarrow x \ge 2$. **Answer: (b).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Solve and represent on a number line:
$5x - 3 \ge 3x - 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2x \ge -2 \Rightarrow x \ge -1$. Closed circle at $-1$, arrow right.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Solve the system: $3x + 4y \le 60,\
x + 3y \le 30,\ x, y \ge 0$. List the corner points.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The corners are intersections of pairs of boundary lines.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Slanted lines meet: $3x + 4y = 60$, $x + 3y = 30$. Multiply second by 3:
$3x + 9y = 90$. Subtract: $5y = 30$, so $y = 6$, $x = 30 - 18 = 12$.

Vertices: $(0, 0), (20, 0), (12, 6), (0, 10)$.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** A workshop produces tables and chairs. A table
needs $2$ hours of carpentry and $1$ hour of finishing. A chair needs
$1$ hour of carpentry and $3$ hours of finishing. The workshop has $40$
hours of carpentry and $50$ hours of finishing per week. Write the
inequalities and sketch the feasible region. Identify all corner points.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let $x$ = tables, $y$ = chairs. Two resource constraints, plus
non-negativity.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Constraints: $2x + y \le 40$, $x + 3y \le 50$, $x \ge 0$, $y \ge 0$.

Boundary lines:

$2x + y = 40$: passes through $(0, 40)$ and $(20, 0)$.

$x + 3y = 50$: passes through $(0, 50/3)$ and $(50, 0)$.

Their intersection: from $2x + y = 40$, $y = 40 - 2x$. Sub:
$x + 3(40 - 2x) = 50 \Rightarrow x + 120 - 6x = 50 \Rightarrow -5x = -70
\Rightarrow x = 14, y = 12$.

**Corner points:** $(0, 0), (20, 0), (14, 12), (0, 50/3)$.

The feasible region is a quadrilateral.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Ch 5 LA "Solve graphically the
system $2x + y \le 24$, etc." matches the 5-mark item.
**Class 12 / JEE pointer:** this chapter is the foundation of **linear
programming** in Class 12.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Inequalities behave like equations — except for one rule.** Multiplying or dividing both sides by a *negative* number flips $<$ to $>$ (and $\le$ to $\ge$). Forget this once and the rest of the answer is wrong.
2. **A two-variable inequality is a half-plane.** Draw the boundary line; pick a test point (the origin if it isn't on the line); shade the side that satisfies the inequality. Strict inequalities get a *dashed* boundary, weak ones get a *solid* boundary.
3. **A system of inequalities is the intersection of half-planes.** This [feasible region](/glossary.qmd#feasible-region) is always convex, and its corner points are where Class 12 linear programming will look for the optimum.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Multiplying both sides by anything keeps the inequality."** Wrong.
   Multiplying by a *negative* flips the sign. Multiplying by a variable
   of unknown sign is forbidden without case analysis.
2. **"$\le$ and $<$ give the same picture."** They give the same shaded
   region but a different boundary: $\le$ is solid, $<$ is dashed.
   Boards mark this distinction.
3. **"$|x| < c$ means $x < c$."** No. $|x| < c$ means $-c < x < c$ (for
   $c > 0$). The absolute value translates into a *bounded* interval,
   not a half-line.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you solve a one-variable inequality including a negative
   coefficient without notes?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 6 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you sketch a system of two or three inequalities and identify the
   corner points?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Permutations and Combinations](../06-permutations-combinations/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item — Class 12 LP depends
  on this.
- **0–1 yes:** sketch ten more two-inequality systems by hand.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Class 10 — Pair of Linear Equations](../../tier-cbse-10-class-10/03-pair-linear-equations/index.qmd)** —
  the boundary lines are exactly two-variable linear equations.
- **[Linear Inequalities (T2)](../../tier-2-prealgebra/linear-inequalities/index.qmd)** —
  one-variable warm-up.

### What this unlocks

- **[Class 12 — Linear Programming](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/12-linear-programming/index.qmd)** —
  optimise a linear function over a feasible region built exactly as
  this chapter teaches.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 11, Chapter 5 — Linear Inequalities** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised). Sections 5.1–5.4: algebraic and graphical solutions in
  one and two variables; systems.
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t2.linear-inequalities`](../../tier-2-prealgebra/linear-inequalities/index.qmd).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Support Vector Machines define a *separating half-plane*
between two classes — exactly the picture of a linear inequality. The
"margin" is the distance from data points to the boundary.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / Operations Research.** Every diet, transportation, and
production-planning problem is a system of linear inequalities plus an
objective. The simplex method (Dantzig, 1947) walks the corner points
to find the optimum.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

Touchstones: NRICH (the budget word problem is a low-floor entry to the
two-variable case), Russian school (Discover problems force the
sign-flip rule before it is named), Bhāskara (verse).
:::
